perturbation.original_text: MORPHO shall pay all charges under this Agreement, including the total Annual Maintenance Charge, within thirty (30) days after receipt of a valid invoice from XIMAGE.
perturbation.changed_text: MORPHO shall pay all charges under this Agreement, including the total Annual Maintenance Charge, within sixty (60) days after receipt of a valid invoice from XIMAGE.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment term from 30 days to 60 days after invoice receipt can introduce legal complexities. While seemingly more lenient, extending payment terms without proper consideration might violate prompt payment laws in certain jurisdictions. The original agreement does not specify the jurisdiction, defaulting to United States law. Many states have prompt payment laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Prompt Payment Statutes for Private Works
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5 mandates specific payment timelines for home improvement contracts. While this agreement is not strictly a home improvement contract, the principle of prompt payment is relevant. Extending the payment period to 60 days could be construed as an unreasonable delay, potentially conflicting with the spirit of California's prompt payment expectations, especially if the service relates to software used in facilities within California.

PROFESSIONS AND VOCATIONS GENERALLY [5000 - 9998.11] ( Heading of Division 3 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 30. ) CHAPTER 9. Contractors [7000 - 7191] ( Chapter 9 added by Stats. 1939, Ch. 37. ) ARTICLE 10. Home Improvement Business [7150 - 7170] ( Article 10 added by Stats. 1961, Ch. 1021. ) 7159.5. This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor, whether a general contractor or a specialty contractor, that is licensed or subject to be licensed pursuant to this chapter with regard to the transaction. (a) Failure by the licensee or a person subject to be licensed under this chapter, or by their agent or salesperson, to comply with the following provisions is cause for discipline: (1) The contract shall be in writing and shall include the agreed contract amount in dollars and cents. The contract amount shall include the entire cost of the contract, including profit, labor, and materials, but excluding finance charges. (2) If there is a separate finance charge between the contractor and the person contracting for home improvement, the finance charge shall be set out separately from the contract amount. (3) If a downpayment will be charged, the d
perturbation.justification: The modified text extends the payment term from 30 to 60 days. The perturbation explanation suggests this might violate prompt payment laws, especially without specifying jurisdiction. The Law Explanation points to California Business and Professions Code Section 7159.5, emphasizing the principle of prompt payment. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically BPC 7159.5, states, 'This section applies to all home improvement contracts, as defined in Section 7151.2, between an owner or tenant and a contractor...'. While the agreement isn't explicitly a home improvement contract, the change introduces a potential conflict with the spirit of prompt payment, especially if the service relates to software used in facilities within California and thus potentially subject to such regulations. The extension introduces a legal contradiction by potentially conflicting with prompt payment principles, making the longer payment term potentially unenforceable or illegal.

perturbation.original_text: XIMAGE agrees to respond to any telephone call made, within 30 minutes.
perturbation.changed_text: XIMAGE agrees to respond to any telephone call made, within 6 hours.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the response time from 30 minutes to 6 hours for telephone calls might breach reasonable expectations of responsiveness, especially if the system is critical infrastructure. This could violate industry standards or regulations requiring prompt responses in emergency situations, such as those related to security systems.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Alarm system regulations regarding response times
perturbation.law_explanation: While this is an older agreement, current alarm system regulations (e.g., Illinois Alarm System Act) often require specific response times for alarm companies. Delaying the response time to 6 hours contradicts the intent of these regulations, which aim to ensure timely responses to security threats and emergencies. The software maintained may be related to alarm systems.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Home Legislation & Laws Senate House My Legislation Site Map Bills & Resolutions Compiled Statutes Public Acts Legislative Reports IL Constitution Legislative Guide Legislative Glossary Search By Number (example: HB0001) Search Tips Search By Keyword Search Tips Advanced Search Illinois Compiled Statutes Back to Act Listing Public Acts Search Guide Disclaimer Printer-Friendly Version Information maintained by the Legislative Reference Bureau Updating the database of the Illinois Compiled Statutes (ILCS) is an ongoing process. Recent laws may not yet be included in the ILCS database, but they are found on this site as Public Acts soon after they become law. For information concerning the relationship between statutes and Public Acts, refer to the Guide . Because the statute database is maintained primarily for legislative drafting purposes, statutory changes are sometimes included in the statute database before they take effect. If the source note at the end of a Section of the statutes includes a Public Act that has not yet taken effect, the version of the law that is currently in effect may have already been removed from the database and you should refer to that Public Act to see the changes made to the current law. ( ) 410 ILCS 270/1 (410 ILCS 270/1) Sec. 1. Short title; references to Act. (a) Short title. This Act may be cited as the Reducing the Risk of Skin Cancer and Excessive UV Exposure in Children Act. (b) References to Act. This Act may be referred to as the SUNucate Law. (Source: P.A. 100-1176, eff. 1-11-19.) 410 ILCS 270/5 (410 ILCS 270/5) Sec. 5. Definition. In this Act, "school personnel" means any employee of a school. (Source: P.A. 100-1176, eff. 1-11-19.) 410 ILCS 270/10 (410 ILCS 270/10) Sec. 10. Purpose. The General Assembly finds and declares the following: (1) Many children are exposed to ultraviolet (UV) radiation due to suboptimal sunscreen use and high rates of sunburning and are therefore at risk of excessive UV exposure, which could lead to skin cancer development. It is a high priority to ensure that children can use sunscreen and sun-protective clothing when outdoors. (2) News outlets have reported that some schools do not allow children to bring or use sunscreen without a prescription due to medication bans and fears of legal ramifications. (3) The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention believe that school policies that prohibit hats or student possession of sunscreen can create barriers to the use of important sun protection methods. (4) The United States Preventive Services Task Force recommends educating children, adolescents, and young adults on the dangers of sun exposure to reduce the risk of skin cancer. (5) It is in the public's interest that schools set policies that include education on sun exposure and encourage our youth to use sun protection, including sunscreen and sun-protective clothing. (Source: P.A. 100-1176, eff. 1-11-19.) 410 ILCS 270/15 (410 ILCS 270/15) Sec. 15. Sun-protective measures in schools and youth camps. (a) A student may possess and use a topical sunscreen product while on school property or at a school-sponsored event or activity without a physician's note or prescription if the product is approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration for over-the-counter use for the purpose of limiting ultraviolet light-induced skin damage. (b) A participant in a youth camp may possess and use a topical sunscreen product while attending the camp without a physician's note or prescription if the product is approved by the United States Food and Drug Administration for over-the-counter use for the purpose of limiting ultraviolet light-induced skin damage. (c) A school district or youth camp operator may allow school or youth camp personnel to assist students or participants in applying a topical sunscreen product with parental permission. (d) Except for willful or wanton misconduct, school personnel may not be held liable in a criminal or civil action for application
perturbation.justification: The change in response time from 30 minutes to 6 hours creates a legal contradiction. The LLM explanation states that current alarm system regulations (e.g., Illinois Alarm System Act) often require specific response times for alarm companies, and the changed text contradicts the intent of these regulations. While Scraped Law Snippet 1 doesn't directly address alarm system response times, the Law Explanation indicates that such regulations exist and that a 6-hour delay would contradict their purpose of ensuring timely responses to security threats.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that MORPHO require the attendance of an XIMAGE engineer on site, XIMAGE shall upon such a demand (either written or verbal) make their best efforts to dispatch the engineer as expeditiously as possible. XIMAGE undertake to have an engineer on site in Kuwait within 72 hours from the time the request was made, exclusive of the time required to arrange for travel and obtain the required documents, and the actual travel time itself.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event that MORPHO require the attendance of an XIMAGE engineer on site, XIMAGE will dispatch an engineer at their convenience. XIMAGE will make commercially reasonable efforts to have an engineer on site in Kuwait within 14 days from the time the request was made, exclusive of the time required to arrange for travel and obtain the required documents, and the actual travel time itself.
perturbation.explanation: Extending the on-site arrival timeframe from 72 hours to 14 days and changing "best efforts" to "commercially reasonable efforts" introduces significant ambiguity and potential breach of contract issues. If the software is critical and downtime costly, such a delay could be deemed unreasonable. Furthermore, weakening the obligation from 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts' reduces the level of commitment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While not a specific statute with a hard deadline, the change violates the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing present in all contracts under the Uniform Commercial Code (UCC § 1-304). Replacing a specific 72-hour commitment with a vague 'commercially reasonable efforts' and a 14-day window allows for undue delay, potentially frustrating the purpose of the agreement, especially if the software malfunction causes significant business disruption. The original text's specificity implies a commitment to rapid response, which the modification undermines.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The original contract specified a 72-hour response time, while the changed text extends this to 14 days and weakens the obligation from 'best efforts' to 'commercially reasonable efforts'. This contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, as it allows for undue delay, potentially frustrating the agreement's purpose. Snippet 1, 'Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement,' supports the argument that the modification undermines the original agreement's intent for a rapid response, thus contradicting the obligation of good faith.
